coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots based fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of thiosulfate anion and oxidative compounds
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yuhong
Hu, Yu
Weng, Weiyang
Chang, Shuai
Xu, Hu
Li, Dan
Li, Dawei
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the huge threat to human health and environment from thiosulfate anion (S2O3 2−) widely used in various fields, the development of an effective detection method for S2O3 2− is urgently desired. Herein, we developed an iodine (I2) mediated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (I2-mediated N-GQDs) as a fluorescence “off-on” probe for S2O3 2− by iodimetry reaction oriented strategy. Firstly, I2 interacted with N-GQDs to form non-fluorescent N-GQDs/I2 complex due to the inner filter effect (IFE). In the presence of S2O3 2−, the iodimetry reaction between I2 and S2O3 2− disintegrated N-GQDs/I2 complex and re-switched on the quenched photoluminscence of N-GQDs. The I2-mediated N-GQDs nanoprobe with a limit of detection down to 93 pM has been successfully used to sense S2O3 2- in biological fluids and living cells. Moreover, the I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2− nanoplatform could further detect oxidative compounds such as hydrogen dioxide, dichromate, permanganate, iron ion (III), copper ion (II), and cerium ion (IV).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Anion detection
Thiosulfate anion
Oxidative compounds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of S2O3 2− by I2-mediated N-GQDs
PL response of N-GQDs to I2

PL recovery of I2-mediated N-GQDs by S2O3 2
−
Detection of S2O3 2− based on I2-mediated N-GQDs
Probing S2O3 2− by I2-mediated N-GQDs in real water samples, biological fluids, and living cells
Extending application of I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2− nanoplatform for oxidative compounds

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The PL spectra of N-GQDs (0.1 mg/mL) by adding I2 from 0 to 4 nM (the insert represents the PL response of N-GQDs towards different I2 concentrations). (b) The subsequent PL recovery of I2-mediated N-GQDs by S2O3 2− (b). (c) The photos corresponding to the above processes (a) with I2 (4 nM) and (b) with the subsequent addition of S2O3 2− (10 nM) under UV/ambient light irradiation.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of I2 (black line), excitation (blue line, λ em =545 nm) and emission spectra (red line, λ ex =470 nm) of N-GQDs. (b) Observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiency of I2 toward N-GQDs PL. (c) Normalized PL decay curves of N-GQDs (black line) together with adding I2 (red line) and subsequent adding S2O3 2− (blue line).

(a) Linear plot of PL response of I2-mediated N-GQDs towards S2O3 2−. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. (b) The selectivity of I2 (4 nM)-mediated N-GQDs (0.1 mg/mL) to S2O3 2- (10 nM) over other competing components (all at 8 μM except for S2- and SO3 2- at 2 μM). The inserted photos display the corresponding fluorescence images under UV light irradiation. (c) The effect of co-presence of other components on I2 (4 nM)-mediated N-GQDs (0.1 mg/mL) PL response. Bar A represents only the result of I2-mediated N-GQDs; bar B stands for the result of co-existence of other components (all at 8 μM except for S2- and SO3 2- at 2 μM) without S2O3 2-; bar C corresponds to the result upon adding S2O3 2- (10 nM) alone; bar D exhibits the result of the co-existence of S2O3 2- (10 nM) and other competing components (all at 8 μM except for S2- and SO3 2- at 2 μM). The right photos represent the corresponding fluorescent images of tested suspension under UV light.

Fluorescence microscopic images under bright field (BF), UV light, and merged images of RAW cells incubated with I2 (4 nM)-mediated N-GQDs (0.1 mg/mL) for 0.5 h and then further exposed to S2O3 2− (0, 2, 4, and 10 nM) for 0.5 h at 37 °C.

(a) The PL response of I2 (4 nM)-mediated N-GQDs (0.1 mg/mL)/S2O3 2− (10 nM) to adding H2O2 from 0 to 10 nM (the insert represents the PL response F/F0 of I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2− system towards different I2 concentrations). (b) Recycling tests of N-GQDs/S2O3 2− system for H2O2. (The adding amount of H2O2 and S2O3 2− is 10 and 8 nM, respectively).

Schematic illustration of the probing mechanism of I2-mediated N-GQDs for S2O3 2− and further detection of oxidative compounds including H2O2. The figures represent the PL “off-on” response for S2O3 2− and PL “on-off” response for H2O2, respectively. The photos stand for the fluorescence images of corresponding systems and the cellular imaging of S2O3 2- by this probe.
Detection of S2O3 2− by I2-mediated N-GQDs in real water samples and biological fluids.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots based fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of thiosulfate anion and oxidative compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Applied Catalysis, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai, 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aResearch Institute of Applied Catalysis, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai, 201418, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory for Advanced Materials, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An I2-mediated N-GQDs was developed to fluorescent probes for S2O3 2− and oxidative compounds such as H2O2 via iodimetry reaction oriented strategy. This nanoprobe with the excellent selectivity and sensitivity (93 pM) has been successfully applied in biological fluids and living cells for S2O3 2−.

Owing to the huge threat to human health and environment from thiosulfate anion (S2O3 2−) widely used in various fields, the development of an effective detection method for S2O3 2− is urgently desired. Herein, we developed an iodine (I2) mediated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (I2-mediated N-GQDs) as a fluorescence “off-on” probe for S2O3 2− by iodimetry reaction oriented strategy. Firstly, I2 interacted with N-GQDs to form non-fluorescent N-GQDs/I2 complex due to the inner filter effect (IFE). In the presence of S2O3 2−, the iodimetry reaction between I2 and S2O3 2− disintegrated N-GQDs/I2 complex and re-switched on the quenched photoluminscence of N-GQDs. The I2-mediated N-GQDs nanoprobe with a limit of detection down to 93 pM has been successfully used to sense S2O3 2- in biological fluids and living cells. Moreover, the I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2− nanoplatform could further detect oxidative compounds such as hydrogen dioxide, dichromate, permanganate, iron ion (III), copper ion (II), and cerium ion (IV).

ce:section.ce:para: Anion detection has substantially attracted much interest for several decades due to the wide use in agriculture, industry, environment, and medicine etc [1–3]. Thiosulfate anion (S2O3 2−) as an inorganic anion has been widely used in paper making, pesticide, sewage disposal, and textile etc. S2O3 2− involved chemical reactions can produce many toxic sulfur containing compounds such as sulfur dioxide, hydrogen sulfide, and sulfide etc [4]. In addition, excessive dosage of thiosulfate as a drug for calciphylaxis also causes emesis, dizziness, and anorexia etc [5]. Therefore, there is an urgent desire to develop an efficient method for quantitative S2O3 2− detection in natural and biological environments. So far, many methods including traditional manual titration, chromatography [6,7], postcolumn fluorescence [8,9] or electrochemical method [10], and colorimetry [11,12] have been developed for quantitative analysis of S2O3 2−. However, the demand of time-consuming, high cost, and sophisticated equipment as well as the relatively low sensitivity limit their wide application.

Fluorescence method without the help of chromatography seems a promising alternative for the rapid detection due to the simple operation, high sensitivity, and low cost etc. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely studied due to their unique optical properties [13,14]. As an outstanding emitting material, GQDs have been developed to probe inorganic ions, small organic molecules, and biomolecules such as Hg2+, Al3+, I−, PO4 3-, ascorbic acid, amino acids, dopamine, human serum, urine, aspergillus, antibiotic, pesticide and so on [15–23]. For instance, Qiu et al. developed a dual mode detection method for Hg2+ by means of the synergistic effect of trace nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and Hg2+ in accelerating the coordination rate of Mn2+ and a porphyrin, in which the absorption red-shift of porphyrin and the fluorescence enhancement of N-GQDs could be observed [22]. However, there isn’t any report focusing on S2O3 2- detection by GQDs. As far as we know, there is one report focusing on nanoparticles-based fluorescent probe for S2O3 2- by using silver nanoclusters embedded hydrogel system (AgNCs-PBH) via fluorescence quenching mode [24]. Recently, we reported a yellow light-emitting N-GQDs-based probe for inorganic phosphate anion by traditional chemical reaction [25]. Herein, we further extended the fluorescence application of N-GQDs for S2O3 2- and oxidative compounds by means of iodimetry reaction oriented strategy. The probe utilized iodine (I2) to behave as a mediator. The sensing principle of I2-mediated N-GQDs for S2O3 2- was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Facile access to the surface of N-GQDs of I2 quenched the photoluminescence (PL) of N-GQDs via inner filter effect (IFE). In the presence of S2O3 2-, the quantitative iodimetry reaction between I2 and S2O3 2- consumed the adsorbed I2 and simultaneously vanished the IFE. As a result, the quenched PL of N-GQDs was switched on. Furthermore, the iodimetry reaction involved I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2- nanoplatform could be further utilized to probe oxidative compounds including hydrogen dioxide (H2O2), dichromate (Cr2O7 2-), permanganate (MnO4-), iron ion (Fe3+), copper ion (Cu2+), and cerium ion (Ce4+) by fluorescence “on-off” mode.

Sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4), ethanol (EtOH), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH 6.0), sodium iodide (NaI), sodium fluoride (NaF), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium bromide (NaBr), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), cerium ammonium nitrate (Ce(NH4)2(NO3)6), potassium chloride (KCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3), copper chloride (CuCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium sulfide (Na2S), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium persulfate (Na2(SO4)2), chlorine (Cl2), bromine (Br2), iodine (I2), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3), acetic acid (99 %), julolidine (C12H15N), human blood serum, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were bought from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China) and were all analytical grade. RAW cells (RAW 264.7 (leukemia cells in mouse macrophage)) were purchased from MoXi Biotech. Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were recorded on 759S and F97pro spectrophotometers (Leng Guang, Shanghai). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a NICOLET IZ10 spectrometer with KBr pellet. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was operated on a JEOL JEM-2100 at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV to obtain high-resolution images of individual GQDs. The fluorescence lifetime study was carried out by an Edinburgh Instruments Life Spec-ps spectrometer equipped with a Hamamatsu C8898 picosecond light pulser. The excitation light (470 nm) was obtained from a 470 nm laser with frequency of 1.0 MHz. For bioimaging, fluorescence images were recorded using a dark-field microscope (eclipse TiU, Nikon, Japan) equipped with a dark-field condenser (0.8 < NA < 0.95), a 40×objective lens (NA = 0.8), and a true color digital camera, which were used to capture the color cell images. Illumination was provided by a 100 W halogen lamp, and a mercury lamp was used as the excitation source of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of S2O3 2− by I2-mediated N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
N-GQDs was prepared and characterized according to our previous report [24]. All PL measurements were carried out in the same conditions: the slits width was set at 10 nm for excitation and emission tests (λ ex =470 nm). The emission spectra were collected in the range from 480 to 700 nm. The PBS buffer (pH 6.0, 10 mM) is used to keep the constant of pH in samples. A typical detection procedure for S2O3 2
− is as follows: 5 μL of purified N-GQDs suspension (0.1 mg/mL) was added to 2.0 mL of PBS buffer in a 10 mL quartz cell with stirring and then I2 (4 nM) was added to the quartz cell. The PL spectrum was recorded subsequently and the PL intensity was set to F0. Due to the fast diffusion of I2, the PL of N-GQDs was almost instantaneously quenched. Introducing 1 μL of S2O3 2 - stock solution (16 μM) into the above suspension, the PL was then scanned and regarded as F. A series of F/F0 was obtained by changing the concentration of S2O3 2 - in samples. The working plot was further obtained based on these F/F0 values. For screening S2O3 2- in human serum and urine samples (collected from the healthy male), which were used as the carrier for N-GQDs probe and analyte, the specific S2O3 2- content was artificially regulated.

For real samples, water and urine samples were collected from the Xinnong River flowing through the new campus of Shanghai University of Technology in Fengxian District in Shanghai and the healthy male, respectively. To ensure the suitable pH circumstance, the real samples were firstly diluted one time by PBS (10 mM, pH 6.0). The detection process was as same as described above except for the replace of PBS by the above 1:1 diluted real samples. Direct analysis of Direct analysis of S2O3 2− in river samples by I2-mediated N-GQDs could give an evident PL restoration. The accurate S2O3 2- level could be determined by the standard addition method. As for other samples (human blood serum and urine) without no any PL enhancement by direct analysis, the specific S2O3 2- levels could be obtained by artificial regulation.

RAW cells were cultured in Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, GIBCO) and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI)-1640 medium (GIBCO) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Sigma), streptomycin (100 μg/mL), and penicillin (100 μg/mL) in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). After 24 h, the cells were collected and then incubated in the solution with 2.5 mL media, 0.5 mL alcohol, and 0.1 mg/mL of I2-mediated N-GQDs for 1 h. Then, the samples were slightly washed by DMEM. Finally, three concentrations of S2O3 2− (2, 4, and 10 nM) were separately added into the above cells solution and incubated for 0.5 h for bright field and fluorescence imaging later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of N-GQDs to I2
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the promising optical properties of N-GQDs [24], herein we only reported the new fluorescence application for S2O3 2
− detection. Firstly, it was worth noting that the PL of N-GQDs was dramatically sensitive to neutral I2. The easy access of I2 to N-GQDs surface by means of the large N-GQDs surface facilitated the strong interaction between them. As a result, the PL of N-GQDs was significantly quenched. With the successive increase of I2 concentration, the PL intensity of N-GQDs at 545 nm was gradually decreased (Fig. 1 a). By plotting the term F/F0 versus I2 concentration, a downward curve could be easily obtained (F0 and F stand for the PL intensities of N-GQDs suspension in the absence and presence of I2, respectively). This demonstrates that the higher I2 concentration, the more extent of PL quenching of N-GQDs can be achieved. In addition, the instantaneous PL quenching effect shew the ultrafast reaction kinetic characteristic between N-GQDs and I2 (Fig. S1 in Supporting information).

For the PL quenching of N-GQDs by I2, the possibility of N-GQDs aggregation induced PL quenching was firstly ruled out since no evident aggregation occurred after adding I2 into N-GQDs suspension (Fig. S2). The possible mechanism could be ascribed to the IFE due to the effective spectra overlap between absorption band of I2 and the PL band of N-GQDs (Fig. 2 a) [26]. The IFE was further corrected according to the previous method [27]. From the suppressed PL efficiency of I2 towards N-GQDs, the evident PL quenching of N-GQDs was partly attributed to the IFE between I2 and N-GQDs (Fig. 2b and Table S1). The PL quenching of N-GQDs by I2 was further investigated by time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry, which is usually a useful tool to distinguish the dynamic or static quenching [28] since exciton lifetime changes proportionally with the quencher concentration for dynamic quenching and keeps constant in static quenching [29,30]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the lifetime of excited state of N-GQDs before (6.33 ns) and after (5.87 ns) adding I2 didn’t exhibit the evident difference (the details about lifetimes were comprised in Table S2), which implied the PL quenching maybe due to static quenching. By means of the Stern-Volmer equation, the quenching rate constant (K q) was estimated to be 6.17 × 1017 L∙mol−1∙s-1 based on the N-GQDS lifetime and quenching constant. The K q value is much higher than 2.0 × 1010 L∙mol-1∙s-1 (the up-limited rate constant for two components fluorescence quenching system) [31]. Both PL lifetime and K q value demonstrate that the PL quenching of N-GQDS by I2 is also partly ascribed to the formation of non-fluorescent N-GQDs/I2 complex (i.e. static quenching) through strong interaction. The binding constant between N-GQDs and I2 was found to be about 7.3 × 107 M−1 by Benesi-Hildebrand method [32]. The strong interaction was also reflected by an evident color changes under ambient and UV light irradiation (Fig. 1c). With the increase of I2 concentration (0−4 nM), the absorbance of N-GQDs/I2 complex at 460 nm was gradually increased from 0.49 to 0.86 (Fig. S3a). Moreover, I2 also exhibted the excellent quenching ability to N-GQDs PL (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title:
PL recovery of I2-mediated N-GQDs by S2O3 2
−
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of S2O3 2− into I2-mediated N-GQDs suspension could induce dramatically the PL recovery (Fig. 1b). This phenomenon could be attributed to the quantitative iodimetry reaction between I2 and S2O3 2−. The fast consumption of I2 by S2O3 2- (Fig. S5) led to the decrease of I2 content, which significantly destroyed N-GQDs/I2 complex and further weakened the IFE effect between them. Thus, the obvious PL restoration was observed. The PL intensity of I2-mediated N-GQDs was gradually increased with the S2O3 2− concentration increasing successively from 0 to 10 nM (Fig. 1b). An overall 45-fold PL enhancement could be clearly observed. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the probe system was also reverted to the incipient value (Fig. S3b). In addition, the lifetime of N-GQDs also kept the original level (6.23 ns) after adding S2O3 2− (Table S2). Therefore, the optical response of I2-mediated N-GQDs to S2O3 2- could be developed to a fluorescence “off-on” probe for S2O3 2−. In fact, the similar fluorescence “off-on” strategy has been used for oxygenated anion including ClO- [33], PO4 3- [24], HSO3- [34] etc. As far as we know, this work is the first report about detecting S2O3 2- by using N-GQDs as an optical reporter. More importantly, our iodimetry reaction oriented nanoplatform further verifies the practicability of the combination of novel nanomaterial with classic reaction, which will greatly expand the applications of classic chemical reactions in modern analytical chemistry [24,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S2O3 2− based on I2-mediated N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a fluorescent probe, it is necessary to optimize the experimental variables in the detection process. The effects of two experimental parameters, pH value of N-GQDs suspension and I2 concentration on the performance were studied. Firstly, the effect of pH on the detection performance for S2O3 2− by I2-mediated N-GQDs was investigated. It was found that there was a maximum PL enhancement factor at pH 6.0. (Fig. S6). Actually, too low or high pH values seems unsuitable to provide an appropriate circumstance facilitating the reaction between I2 and S2O3 2-. S2O3 2- prefers to react with proton under acidic conditions to afford S, SO2, and SO4 2-. While I2 can undergo the disproportionation reaction to provide iodate and iodine ions under basic conditions. As expected, a maximum PL enhancement factor appears at near neutral conditions (pH 6.0 in this work). Thus, pH 6.0 was used as the pH value in subsequent detection. Furthermore, I2 concentration was found to be another important factor affecting the PL enhancement effect. Fig. S7 showed the effect of I2 concentration on PL enhancement by S2O3 2-. For five different I2 concentrations, 4 nM gave the best PL enhancement. Ideally, the added I2 (4 nM) can just quench fully the PL of N-GQDs via IFE between N-GQDs and I2. Too less I2 will absolutely result in a poor PL quenching, which in turn is really difficult to achieve a satisfactory PL enhancement effect. On the contrary, although too much I2 can result in an excellent PL quenching, excess I2 will inevitably consume more S2O3 2-, which no doubtly will affect the overall sensitivity of the probe. Therefore, 4 nM of I2 concentration was used in subsequent tests.

The specific detection performance of I2-mediated N-GQDs on S2O3 2− was further evaluated. Under the optimized conditions, F/F0 is linearly related to S2O3 2−concentration ranging from 2.0–10 nM with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.996 (Fig. 3 a). The limit of detection (LOD) of I2-mediated N-GQDs for S2O3 2- was calculated to be 93 pM according to IUPAC 3σ criterion. The lower LOD can partly be ascribed to the excellent PL quenching ability of I2, exclusive reaction ability between I2 and S2O3 2- as well as the high PL quantum yield (0.53) of N-GQDs [24]. Our I2-mediated N-GQDs probe shew the higher sensitivity, which is superior to most of other optical S2O3 2− sensors (Table S3). The relative standard deviation (RSD) for three repeated tests with 3.4 nM of S2O3 2- was 1.74 %, indicating the excellent reproducibility of I2-mediated N-GQDs probe for S2O3 2−. The more interesting thing about this probe system was the repeated use by another round of adding I2 followed by S2O3 2− addition even without any purification treatment. An overall 7 cycles can be conducted successively without a significant decrease of PL feature (Fig. S8). This implies that the present I2-mediated N-GQDs probe for S2O3 2- is considerably suitable for the real time, on-site, and reusable detection.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of I2-mediated N-GQDs to S2O3 2−, other negatively charged anions including oxygenated carbonate (CO3 2-), sulfate (SO4 2-), bicarbonate (HCO3-), persulfate ((SO4)2 2-), phosphate (PO4 3-), nitrate (NO3-), nitrite (NO2-), and sulfite (SO3 2-) were also investigated. As shown in Fig.3b, except for S2O3 2−, other anions such as SO4 2−, CO3 2-, HCO3-, (SO4)2 2-, I-, F-, NO3-, Cl-, Br-, PO4 3-, NO2-, didn’t almost trigger the effective PL enhancement even with 800 times higher concentration of S2O3 2−. However, S2− and SO3 2− could more or less trigger the negligible PL enhancement at 200 times higher concentration of S2O3 2−, which can be ascribed to the moderate reducibility of S2- and SO3 2-. On the contrary, a low concentration of S2O3 2− (10 nM) could produce significantly the PL enhancement effect with distinct yellow fluorescence, indicating the selectivity of I2-mediated N-GQDs towards S2O3 2− is really remarkable. To further validate the excellent selectivity of I2-mediated N-GQDs to S2O3 2−, we also evaluated the effect of co-existence of these competing components on PL response of I2-mediated N-GQDs (bar B and D in Fig. 3c). In addition, we also performed experiments containing S2O3 2− only for comparison (bar C in Fig. 3c). These results clearly demonstrate that a very small amount of S2O3 2- will drastically induce the remarkable PL enhancement even at a concentration down to 8 nM whether or not there are other competing components in the suspension (bar C and D in Fig. 3c). The excellent selectivity of I2-mediated N-GQDs for S2O3 2− can be greatly attributed to the exclusive reaction ability between S2O3 2− and I2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing S2O3 2− by I2-mediated N-GQDs in real water samples, biological fluids, and living cells
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the actual performance of I2-mediated N-GQDs for S2O3 2−, the content of S2O3 2- in natural water, human blood serum, and urine samples were determined and the results were listed in Table 1 . As expected, the detection data were good agreement with the amount added artificially. The satisfactory recovery results (98.30–105.2 %) reflected indirectly no significant interference from the common inorganic ions or nutrients existed in these real samples during S2O3 2− detection by I2-mediated N-GQDs. All these results indicate that our I2-mediated N-GQDs probe has excellent operability in detecting S2O3 2− in real water samples and biological fluids.

The cellular imaging of S2O3 2− by I2-mediated N-GQDs was examined on RAW cell line (Fig. 4 ). Live RAW cells stained with our probe for 0.5 h could not exhibit a little fluorescence response. Further incubation with S2O3 2− (2, 4, and 10 nM) for 0.5 h, three groups of RAW cells all displayed the dose-dependent PL “off-on” response indicating the effective in vitro detection ability of I2-mediated N-GQDs probe to S2O3 2−. Comparing to the fluorescence images with low S2O3 2- levels (2 and 4 nM), RAW cells incubated with 10 nM of S2O3 2− gave the strongest PL enhancement. Furthermore, RAW cells showed the excellent probe uptake ability as shown by the strong yellow fluorescence within live cells. Moreover, I2-mediated N-GQDs probe entered mainly the nucleus area where the stronger fluorescence was observable (comparable to that in cytoplasm area). Anyway, these results demonstrate that I2-mediated N-GQDs probe has an excellent cell membrane permeable ability and can be rationally utilized in cellular imaging for S2O3 2−. In addition, the almost entirely accommodation of N-GQDs related probes in nucleus area of live cells implies the excellent targeting feature of N-GQDs-based probes [24]. This will provide a possibility for designing a N-GQDs-based targeted drug delivery systems. The study along this direction is underway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extending application of I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2− nanoplatform for oxidative compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The iodimetry reaction between S2O3 2− and I2 is usually used to quantitatively probe the oxidative compounds by means of the moderate reducibility of I-. Thus, we attempted to validate the possibility of using the above I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2- nanoplatform in probing oxidative compounds. Herein, H2O2 was selected as the model oxidative compound. As mentioned above, I- was quantitatively released into the solution when 8 nM of S2O3 2− was added into the I2-mediated N-GQDs suspension accompanying with the strong PL restoration. The subsequent addition of H2O2 oxidized quickly I- into I2 that in turn interacted immediately with N-GQDs to form N-GQDs/I2 complex and switched on the IFE effect again. As a result, the recovered PL was re-quenched (Fig. 5 a). Moreover, the extent of PL re-quenching was linearly related with H2O2 content (the insert in Fig. 5a). The LOD of I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2- nanoplatform for H2O2 was estimated to be 0.41 nM. Interestingly, the addition of quantitative S2O3 2- into the above PL re-quenched N-GQDs suspension could recovery the N-GQDs PL again. And the cycling of PL quenching (by H2O2) and the subsequent PL recovery (by S2O3 2-) could undergo for at least 4 cycles by alternately adding H2O2 and S2O3 2- (Fig. 5b). In addition, there was no require for purification treatment such as filtration or centrifugation, which is considerably suitable for the different scenario needs (S2O3 2- or H2O2). Similarly, other oxidants including Cr2O7 2-, MnO4-, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Ce4+ also gave the positive results (Fig. S9). All the results demonstrate the I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2- system can also be further developed to serve as a fluorescence “on-off” probe for oxidants.

In summary, we have developed a I2-mediated N-GQDs probe for S2O3 2− detection by iodimetry reaction oriented strategy. This probe can quantitatively detect S2O3 2- with a high selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, I2-mediated N-GQDs have been successfully used to detect S2O3 2- in real samples and living cells. The I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2− nanoplatform also achieves the successful detection of oxidative compounds. The combination between nanomaterial and classic chemical reaction extends greatly the application of classic chemical reactions in modern analytical chemistry.
Conception and design of study: Yuhong Wang; Hu Xu
Acquisition of data: Yu Hu; Weiyang Weng; Shuai Chang
Analysis and/or interpretation of data: Yuhong Wang; Hu Xu; Dan Li; Dawei Li
Drafting original manuscript: Yu Hu; Weiyang Weng

Wide application of thiosulfate anion (S2O3 2−) results in the huge threat to human health and environment. Moreover, the further conversion of S2O3 2- into toxic hydrogen sulfide, sulfide, and sulfur dioxide etc. from various chemical reactions aggravates this threat. Thus, the development of facile, rapid, and repeatedly used detection probe for S2O3 2− is highly desired. To date, most of analytic methods including chromatography, postcolunm fluorescence, and electrochemistry etc. are difficult to realize a highly efficient detection because of the requirement of sophisticated equipment or complicated sample preparation.

In this work, we reported an I2-mediated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (I2-mediated N-GQDs) as a fluorescence “off-on” probe for S2O3 2− by iodimetry reaction oriented strategy. The strong interaction between I2 and N-GQDs led to the formation of non-fluorescent N-GQDs/I2 complex via the inner-filter effect (IFE). Upon adding S2O3 2−, the quantitative iodimetry reaction between S2O3 2− and I2 destroyed the nanostructure of N-GQDs/I2 complex and eliminated simultaneously the IFE. As a result, the weak photoluminescence (PL) of N-GQDs was re-switched on. And I2-mediated N-GQDs/S2O3 2− nanoplatform can further be used to probe oxidative compounds such as hydrogen dioxide, dichromate, and permanganate etc. (Scheme 1). This probe combines artfully nanomaterial (N-GQDs) with the classic iodimetry reaction to realize the highly efficient and reusable detection for S2O3 2− and oxidants. The quantitative iodimetry reaction together with strong interaction between I2 and N-GQDs (K b = 7.3 × 107 M−1) ensures the high selectivity of this nanoprobe to S2O3 2-. The exclusive reactivity of I2 to S2O3 2-, excellent quenching ability of I2 to N-GQDs PL, and high PL quantum yield (0.53) of N-GQDs jointly contribute to the high sensitivity of this nanoprobe to S2O3 2− (LOD down to 93 pM), which was much lower than most of other methods including chromatography and electrochemistry. Furthermore, the reusable feature of this nanoprobe system without any purification treatment simplifies significantly the detection process and improves greatly the detection efficiency, which is considerably suitable for the real time, on-site, and repeated detection. Meanwhile, the successful quantitative analysis and cellular imaging of S2O3 2- by I2-mediated N-GQDs probe in biological fluids and live RAW cells verifies definitely the feasibility of combining novel nanomaterial with the traditional classic chemical reaction once more, which will extend greatly the application of classic chemical reactions in modern analytical chemistry. We believe that this work can appeal the broad readership and be suitable for publication in Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976123) for financial support.
